lines: [00:00:00] And here on stock scanning 101, I'm going to walk you through how to find stocks in real-time.
[00:00:30] That's the topic of today's lesson.
[00:01:00] And I'm also going to teach you how to use scanners to essentially lighten your workload.

[00:01:30] It improves your efficiency. Rather than you having to manually search through the entire market for any needle in a haystack possible stock that's moving, we're going to put the scanners to work.
[00:02:00] Use that technology to search the market for you.
[00:02:30] As a trader, I often say that we're hunters of volatility and managers of risk.
[00:03:00] The scanners are looking for the volatility, we have to manage the risk.
[00:03:30] So once we get an idea off the scanner, then we can implement that in our trading.

[00:04:00] So stock scanning regardless of what strategy you trade, whether you're trading my strategy or you end up trading something different, you're going to need a method of finding opportunities.
[00:04:30] There are certainly some people that use Wall Street Bets, they use Reddit and Twitter and social media to find ideas of what to trade.
[00:05:00] That is not a very effective strategy for long-term success, in my opinion.

[00:05:30] I mean, if it works for someone then that's great, but I haven't known many traders, or really any that I can think of in fact, that have built a successful career just trading stocks that they find out about on social media.
[00:06:00] You kind of need a little bit of a more reliable system for finding good ideas.

[00:06:30] I suppose if you really got pretty good at understanding the themes on social media, you could probably see what's trending and use that to get an idea and then manage your risk on it, but I think that that would be a fairly inconsistent approach.
[00:07:00] Now, other traders who are trading focusing on breaking news, such as me, will subscribe to news feeds.
[00:07:30] They'll subscribe to a Bloomberg terminal, which is $2000 a month, they'll subscribe to other news services, which can be anywhere from $500 to $1,000 or $2000 a month, even higher.
[00:08:00] And I actually found that to be a very difficult way to trade.
[00:08:30] I tried subscribing to those types of platforms.
[00:09:00] And if I open the newsroom here, so we do have a news room, as you can see right here, that's giving you all of these headlines.

[00:09:30] But the fact is, it's really hard to know just by reading them, which of these headlines are really going to be actionable and which ones will have no effect on the share price of the stock.
[00:10:00] So if I just go full screen here and we just look at some of these headlines.
[00:10:30] So this one here, oh I've got to link it, but it's okay.
[00:11:00] So SMX, so this one had a headline right there, and the stock did nothing, absolutely no response.
[00:11:30] SAGT, this one had a headline right here, absolutely nothing.
[00:12:00] So being the first one to get news isn't necessarily helpful.
[00:12:30] Being the first one to see a stock starting to squeeze, now that is a different story.
[00:13:00] So, it's sort of the chicken or the egg, what comes first?
[00:13:30] The stock squeezing or the news headline?

[00:14:00] It is the news headline that comes first, but because there's thousands of news headlines that come out every day, and most of them do not result in stocks making big moves, it's more reliable to wait for the stock to start moving than to look for every stock that has breaking news, if that makes sense.
[00:14:30] So, just this morning for instance, we had this stock ENVB.
[00:15:00] This hit our scanner right up here.
[00:15:30] It hit one of the other scanners a few minutes earlier.
[00:16:00] So, let's see, it was our running up scanner. So it hit the running up scanner probably at just after 8:00 a.m.
[00:16:30] So if we scroll back on this, yeah, it hit the running up scanner at $2.78.
[00:17:00] So if we back this up, we'll go over here.

[00:18:00] So how actionable was it to get this alert here paired with an audio notification that this stock was popping up with breaking news at 8:01 and that was right down here when it was at $2.78?
[00:18:30] That was pretty actionable, right?
[00:19:00] And this ended up going from 2.80 all the way up to a high of 4.40.
[00:19:30] Now, although it came back down, it had breaking news, it met our five pillars of stock selection, and it made sense to focus on that stock.
[00:20:00] Did I trade it? Absolutely. Locked up $12,000 on it.
[00:20:30] Now, could this have gone to $6, $7, $8, $9, $10 a share? Sure, and I would have kept trading it.
[00:21:00] It came back down, a little disappointing, but nonetheless, this is the way I approach trading.
[00:21:30] I'm looking for stocks that are moving.

[00:22:00] So, while it's helpful to have the news feed right here, because some of the stocks that are on the news feed will be a familiar name, or sometimes a stock will be hitting the scanner like ENVB and then we scroll up and we see that there's a news headline that's come out on it.
[00:22:30] I will say that this news feed right here does not pick up every single headline.
[00:23:00] We it's a service that we pay for and it puts headlines into the room specifically on smaller cap stocks.
[00:23:30] So ENVB, the headline was posted right there at 8:01.
[00:24:00] So then if you had already seen ENVB hitting the scanner, well, you could have also pulled up the stock quote window and seen the news.
[00:24:30] If, for whatever reason you missed that, you would have seen in the chat room that news post.
[00:25:00] So in any event, one of the things that I have seen traders try to do is try to be the first one to get breaking news.
[00:25:30] And being the first one to get breaking news is actually not helpful because you don't know what to do with it.
[00:26:00] How do you know, right? Unless you're really familiar with that company, how do you know that headline is good versus bad?
[00:26:30] It's hard to tell.
[00:27:00] And so, for me, it's almost like have you ever been at a stoplight and the light turns green but no one starts driving?
[00:27:30] And you're like, 'Well, wait a second. Am I missing something here? Well, hold up. Maybe I maybe I should wait also.
[00:28:00] Is there like a train? I don't know what's going on.' And then then one person goes and you're like, 'Oh okay, all right. I guess we're good.'
[00:28:30] Right? It's kind of like that.
[00:29:00] So being the first one to get the news, actually not helpful.
[00:29:30] Trying to read every single headline, also not helpful.
[00:30:00] I wait for the stock to start to move, and that's what I need scanners for.
[00:30:30] Now, I would group scanners into kind of two categories, which I'm going to explain here in just a moment.
[00:31:00] But the way I use scans is I observe stocks on the scanners and then I check the five pillars of stock selection.
[00:31:30] So that's pretty straightforward.

[00:32:00] I see a stock like ENVB that hits the scanner right here, and while I could scan strictly for stocks that meet all five pillars of stock selection, what I would be doing is perhaps casting a little bit too small of a net, because sometimes there are stocks that are a little on the outskirts of the five pillars, they're close, and they could come into the five pillars.
[00:32:30] I'll give you an example.
[00:33:00] So let's say we've got a stock, we've got our five pillars.
[00:33:30] Number one, we've got price. 2 and 20, we've got number two up at least 10%, we've got number three relative volume of five, number four news, number five float under 20 million.
[00:34:00] So what if we had, for instance, a stock that was $1.80?
[00:34:30] And everything else checked out.
[00:35:00] Well, if it keeps going up, it'll be above $2, and then it'll fall within the five pillars pretty quickly.
[00:35:30] So it makes sense that I should probably open the filter on my scanners to allow lower-priced stocks.
[00:36:00] What about a stock that's at $25, but it meets all these pillars?
[00:36:30] Well, you know, it might be worth considering that.
[00:37:00] I don't know that I should rule it out, even though I do the best between 2 and 20.
[00:37:30] It's not that I don't make money above 20, so that would probably be okay.
[00:38:00] So then the price filter can be a little bit more open.
[00:38:30] What if a stock meets all these pillars but it's up only 8%?
[00:39:00] Well, if it's moving quickly, it's probably going to be up 10% before you know it and I want to get in early.
[00:39:30] I want to see it early. So maybe that filter's a little lighter.
[00:40:00] What if the relative volume is only three, but volume's coming in?
[00:40:30] Well, before you know it it'll be five. I want to see it soon.
[00:41:00] What if it doesn't have news but it meets all of these and it's moving?
[00:41:30] Well, I probably still want to know because that sounds interesting.
[00:42:00] GameStop didn't have news but it went up as much as it did.
[00:42:30] So these ones I can be a little bit more flexible about.
[00:43:00] The float, I'm a little less flexible about.
[00:43:30] The float, you know, if the float's higher, that's really going to affect all of this.
[00:44:00] It's going to affect because this is such a critical component in our relationship between supply and demand.
[00:44:30] All of these are demand and supply is right here.
[00:45:00] So if one of the characteristics of demand is a little off, I can usually work with that.
[00:45:30] But if the supply is too high, that's usually going to be a no-go.
[00:46:00] So for that reason, with my scans, I have a couple of different scans but one of the ones that I'm the strictest about is float.
[00:46:30] The exception is when we have a stock, we do have a medium float scan which some traders asked for, so we built it out.
[00:47:00] I don't personally use it very much.
[00:47:30] And then we also have the squeeze up 5% and squeeze up 10%.
[00:48:00] So if a stock is moving, even if the float's higher, I'll let it come through the scans, so I'll take a look at it.
[00:48:30] So even though you could filter these scans as tight as you wanted and make it truly only show five pillars, I cast a slightly wider net.
[00:49:00] So that means when a stock hits one of my scanners, I already have a pretty good idea, because I'm watching the scanners all the time.
[00:49:30] So I'm always seeing the floats, the prices, the relative volume, the up percentages.
[00:50:00] I'm seeing all of those things as they're hitting the scanner.
[00:50:30] So it's not like I get a scanner alert and then I have to check all of these things.
[00:51:00] By the time I get to it, I already have a good sense of it.
[00:51:30] And if it looks promising, then I'm going to go check the news, and if there's no news, I might watch it.
[00:52:00] So that brings me to scanner types.
[00:52:30] So I primarily use three different scanner types.
[00:53:00] The first one is small cap high day, which is probably my most commonly used scanner.
[00:53:30] This is looking for stocks that are up a lot today.
[00:54:00] Now how I have it set up, I'm looking for stocks that are up 20% or more today, and that have a market cap of $50 million or less.
[00:54:30] That's my setup for small cap high day.
[00:55:00] So that is a scanner that's showing me stocks that are up big today.
[00:55:30] So this is looking for a lot of volatility, a lot of momentum in a short period of time.
[00:56:00] Now, what I want to see with small cap high day is I want to see that it's up on high relative volume.
[00:56:30] So just because a stock is up 20% doesn't mean it necessarily has high relative volume.
[00:57:00] Sometimes stocks will gap up on a very small amount of volume, and that's not necessarily the best setup.
[00:57:30] But when a stock is up 20% on high relative volume, that tells me there's real conviction behind this move.
[00:58:00] Now, the reason I use small cap high day as my primary scanner is because I want to see stocks that have already moved.
[00:58:30] I want to see that the stock is already up, and I want to find stocks that are still going to continue moving up.
[00:59:00] I'm not trying to find stocks that are about to move.
[00:59:30] I'm trying to find stocks that have already started moving, and I want to get on board with the momentum.
[00:10:00] So that means I'm coming in on what could potentially be a second or third move up.

So I pull that up, I pull up the intraday chart, then I check the daily chart, looking for nearby areas of resistance, recent history of big rejections. And then, if that all looks good, I open up my level 2. I open up my time and sales, I check easy to borrow, hard to borrow, short sale restriction. I check the spreads and the thickness or thinness of the order book. And then I'm ready to take a potential entry based on one of the familiar patterns that we are learning and execute the trades. Typically, it's going to be the first pullback.

01:45 And so that's that's basically what occurs in the moments following an alert off of our scans. Now, as I mentioned, there's sort of two types of scans, well, maybe three. So, we offer two types of scans inside this platform. One of the scans is a scan that provides an alert the moment a stock is hitting the criteria of what that scanner is looking for. And the other is a list of all stocks that currently meet the criteria of what that scanner's looking for. So, if you had a stock for instance, a scanner looking for all stocks under $2, then every single stock under $2 would be on that list. If you're saying, "I want to see a stock that is making a new high of day, price between $2 and $3," then each time that stock makes a new high of day, it's going to register an alert and that's an alert scanner. So, one is a list and the other is creating an alert. And so these two windows certainly do go hand in hand, any scanning window and the stock quote window, and I always want to make sure that they're linked. So, when I click on one of the stocks that are on this list, it'll automatically update my stock quote window, and then similarly, what I'd like to do, of course, is extend that to linking out to some charts. So, I click on LGHL, it updates the stock quote window, it updates the chart, and I could check the daily chart, I could begin doing my due diligence. And of course, one step further on that would be actually connecting it, opening your level 2 window right inside the platform, and then you can also look at the level 2 and see, all right, you know, what do the spreads on this look like.

03:36 So each day, I begin with building a watch list. This is not the watch, this is not a watch list I do at night, it's a watch list I do in the morning, and it's based off my top gainers scanner. So my top gainers scanner right here, top gappers and top gainers, these are stocks right now that are opening higher than the previous day's close. So what's the difference between a top gapper and a top gainer? A gap occurs during the after hours and pre-market trading, it results in the stock opening higher than the previous day's close. So, our gap scanner stops updating at 9:30 a.m. Therefore, it preserves a picture of the market right at 9:30, which was the gap scanner, all the stocks that were gapping higher than the previous day's close. The top gainers scanner works from 4 a.m. to 8 p.m. and is giving you a list of the top gainers in the market versus the previous day's close. So, top gainers scanner keeps updating after 9:30, the top gapper scanner stops updating. So, why would you want the top gapper scanner? Later in the afternoon, you might want to go back and check what were our top gappers at 9:30? If you were using the top gainer scanner, it would, uh, well, it would not be preserving a picture of what the market looked like at the opening bell, and because a lot of traders want to have that picture of how things looked at 9:30, we have the two separate scans. So, one is top gappers, one is top gainers. These are both, however, a list. So, they're giving us a list of all the stocks that meet a certain set of criteria. And you can see on this scanner, we are casting a slightly wider net. We have some higher float stocks on here, we have some higher price stocks on here. 07:08 The running up scanner, so the logic of the high-of-day momentum scanner is that we're receiving market data, and the market data includes every stock that's making a new high of day, because we're getting the time and sales. So, as a stock makes a new high of day, if it makes a new high of day and meets our five pillars of stock selection, and it's moved at least X percent in the last few minutes, we trigger the alert.
